ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spaghetti with meatballs; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta dishes — composite cooked pasta with sauce and meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers composite pasta dishes with sauce and meat; used as the closest defensible category for spaghetti with meatballs in tomato sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for spaghetti with meatballs; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of spaghetti with meatballs weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g cooked spaghetti, 120 g meatballs (3–4 pieces), 70 g tomato sauce, and a light dusting of Parmigiano-Reggiano.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti with Meat Sauce — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists spaghetti with meat sauce (cr_id G00010590) as the closest canonical match for spaghetti polpette. The input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Spaghetti with meat sauce, homemade' (FDC ID 169413) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. All other macros and micronutrients are taken directly from the HPB input record. Detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data are not reported by HPB Foodo for this dish entry and are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a sufficiently close analogue as of 2026-04-16; these fields are set to -1 accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spaghetti polpette (spaghetti with meatballs in tomato sauce) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated using the analogous entry for 'spaghetti, white, boiled 20 min' (GI ~45, low) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The meatball and tomato sauce components are predominantly protein and fat, which do not materially raise the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × net available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × (8.05 − 1.8) g/100g = 21.9 g) / 100 = 9.9; conservative estimate of 15.9 used to account for sauce sugars and portion variability.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 419 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,467 mg sodium, representing 64% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced salt in meatball seasoning and tomato sauce, and omit or minimise Parmigiano-Reggiano topping, which adds a further 150–200 mg sodium per 10 g serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.05 g/100 g with GI ~45 (low); protein from meatballs moderates postprandial glucose response, but portion size is the primary glycemic variable.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes can include this dish in a controlled portion (150–200 g) as part of a balanced meal; substituting wholemeal or legume-based pasta will further lower the GI and improve dietary fibre intake above the 3 g/100 g threshold.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 419 mg/100 g and saturated fat from red-meat meatballs and Parmigiano-Reggiano raise cardiovascular risk; potassium 319 mg/100 g partially offsets sodium impact.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request lean-meat or poultry meatballs, limit portion to 200 g, and avoid added cheese to reduce saturated fat and sodium load in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red-meat meatballs are rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; tomato sauce contains nightshade solanine compounds that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request chicken or turkey meatball substitution, consider omitting tomato sauce if nightshade sensitivity is established, and monitor joint symptoms after consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Polpette is an Italian pasta dish consisting of boiled spaghetti topped with homemade meatballs (polpette), a slow-simmered tomato sauce, and grated Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese. The meatballs are typically made from minced beef or a beef-pork blend, seasoned with garlic, parsley, breadcrumbs, and egg, then pan-fried before being finished in the tomato sauce. The dish delivers a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a tender pasta texture and a slightly acidic, herb-forward sauce. Per 100 g, it provides 51 kcal, 1.41 g protein, 1.48 g fat, and 8.05 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 419 mg. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), making it a moderate glycemic choice when portioned appropriately. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium: a standard 350 g plate delivers approximately 1,467 mg sodium, which is significant for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The red-meat meatballs introduce arachidonic acid, relevant for rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or dairy allergies. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Western and Italian-influenced restaurant settings in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How much sodium is in spaghetti with meatballs?
Can diabetics eat spaghetti with meatballs?

Can I eat spaghetti polpette if I have rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010590) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for white spaghetti (boiled) with documented basis. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a homemade pasta-meatball dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this specific HPB spaghetti polpette preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010590) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a sufficiently close composite analogue as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: full vitamin panel data for this dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database and are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a sufficiently close composite analogue as of 2026-04-16. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: phosphorus, zinc, and selenium data for this dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database and are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for a sufficiently close composite analogue as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat (0.55 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Spaghetti with meat sauce, homemade' (FDC ID 169413) as the closest approved-source analogue; documented in nutrient_source. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on white spaghetti (boiled 20 min) from Atkinson et al. 2008 via University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for spaghetti polpette exists in approved sources. This dish is Italian/Western cuisine served in Singapore; it is not a Singaporean-origin dish, therefore the Singaporean multilingual naming requirement (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) does not apply. The dish name 'Spaghetti Polpette' is Italian; romanized_local is populated accordingly. Halal status is unconfirmed as pork may be present in traditional polpette recipes.
